coredata.dc:title: Synthesis of photoluminescent composite based on graphene quantum dot@ZIF-11: A novel sensor for extremely efficient nano-molar detection of CN–
coredata.dc:creator: Gouran Oorimi, Parisa
Tarlani, Aliakbar
Zadmard, Reza
Muzart, Jacques
coredata.dc:description: This report proposes a simple and very high sensitive photoluminescence sensor for the detection of fatal cyanide ion using a novel composite made from graphene quantum dots (GQDs) as luminescence quantum materials which was encapsulated successfully in zeolitic imidazolate framework)ZIF-11(as a stable porous carrier. Characterization methods such as TGA, TEM, SEM, XRD and IR have been utilized to identify the species. The detection method is based on the significant PL quenching signal of GQDs@ZIF-11 composite by increasing the concentration of cyanide ion. This sensor detected the CN– with wide linear range LDR (0.15 to 30 μM), 0.95 % RSD and excellent limit of detection (LOD) of 14.5 nM. Interference study carried out in the presence of 19 different ions with good sensitivity toward cyanide ion. Also the study was performed on two real cyanide sample solutions which were extracted from apple seeds and bitter almonds. The good selectivity, high sensitivity, stability and wide linear response range made the sensor reliable and valuable compared to other previous ones.
coredata.dcterms:subject: Graphene quantum dots (GQDs)
Luminescence
Metal-organic frameworks (MOFs)
Composite
GQDs@ZIF-11
Cyanide sensor

xocs:item-toc-entry.xocs:item-toc-section-title: Preparation of new composite of GQD@ZIF-11
Optical properties of composite of GQD@ZIF-11
Photoluminescence detection of cyanide ion and competitive titration with different anions

xocs:funding.xocs:funding-agency-matched-string: Chemistry & Chemical Engineering Research Center of Iran

xocs:funding-list.xocs:funding-text: We thank Chemistry & Chemical Engineering Research Center of Iran (CCERCI) for the support of these studies.

ce:caption.ce:simple-para: SEM images of: (a) GQD, (b) ZIF-11 and TEM image of: (c) GQD@ZIF-11.
Thermal gravimetric analysis of GQD, ZIF-11 and GQD@ZIF-11 composite.
Liquid PL spectra of GQD and GQD@ZIF-11 (a,b) and solid PL spectra of GQD, ZIF-11 s and GQD@ZIF-11 (c).

Change in fluorescence spectra of receptor (C = 1 × 10−6M) upon the addition of CN– (0–500 μL) in MeOH (λex = 360 nm) (a). Fluorescence ratio (I0 – I) of receptor at 465 nm upon addition of a particular anion (C = 1 × 10−6M) in MeOH (λex = 360 nm) (b). Linear relationship between fluorescence intensity (ΔI/I0) and concentration from 10 to 500 μL. I0 and I are the PL intensities of receptor at 360 nm excitation in the absence and presence of CN– ions respectively (c). Fluorescence intensity changes of receptor (C = 1 × 10−6M) in MeOH upon addition of various anions (C = 1 × 10−5M) (PO4 3−, CO3 2–, SCN−, NO2 –, ClO3 −, NO3 –, HCO3 –, CH3COO–, H2PO4 −, S2O8 2−, SO4 −, S2O4 2−, S2−, HS−, F−, OH–, Cl−, Br−& I−) in the presence and absence of CN– ions (λex = 360 nm) (d).
Fluorescence spectra of GQD@ZIF-11 composite (0.1 μM) upon addition of extracted cyanide containing solution from different seed solutions (0.3 μM).
Comparison of GQD@ZIF-11 sensor with other sensors for detection of CN–.

row.entry: Probe based on an AIE active cobalt(II) complex
Hf (IV) based metal–organic framework with UiO-66 topology

ce:caption.ce:simple-para: The detection/recovery of CN– in spiked samples.

head.ce:title: Synthesis of photoluminescent composite based on graphene quantum dot@ZIF-11: A novel sensor for extremely efficient nano-molar detection of CN–

ce:affiliation.ce:label: a
ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: Faculty of Chemical Processes Development, Chemistry & Chemical Engineering Research Center of Iran (CCERCI), Tehran, Iran

sa:affiliation.sa:organization: Chemistry & Chemical Engineering Research Center of Iran (CCERCI)

ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: Faculty of Modern Technologies, Chemistry & Chemical Engineering Research Center of Iran (CCERCI), Tehran, Iran

sa:affiliation.sa:organization: Chemistry & Chemical Engineering Research Center of Iran (CCERCI)

ce:affiliation.ce:source-text: Faculty of Modern Technologies, Chemistry & Chemical Engineering Research Center of Iran (CCERCI), Tehran, Iran

ce:affiliation.ce:label: c
ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: Institut de ChimieMoléculaire de Reims, CNRS-Université de Reims Champagne-Ardenne, 51687 Reims Cedex 2, France
ce:affiliation.ce:source-text: Institut de ChimieMoléculaire de Reims, CNRS-Université de Reims Champagne-Ardenne, 51687 Reims Cedex 2, France

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para: Novel photoluminescent GQD@ZIF-11 composite as an effective sensor for extremely efficient nano-molar tracing of CN–.

This report proposes a simple and very high sensitive photoluminescence sensor for the detection of fatal cyanide ion using a novel composite made from graphene quantum dots (GQDs) as luminescence quantum materials which was encapsulated successfully in zeolitic imidazolate framework)ZIF-11(as a stable porous carrier. Characterization methods such as TGA, TEM, SEM, XRD and IR have been utilized to identify the species. The detection method is based on the significant PL quenching signal of GQDs@ZIF-11 composite by increasing the concentration of cyanide ion. This sensor detected the CN– with wide linear range LDR (0.15 to 30 μM), 0.95 % RSD and excellent limit of detection (LOD) of 14.5 nM. Interference study carried out in the presence of 19 different ions with good sensitivity toward cyanide ion. Also the study was performed on two real cyanide sample solutions which were extracted from apple seeds and bitter almonds. The good selectivity, high sensitivity, stability and wide linear response range made the sensor reliable and valuable compared to other previous ones.

ce:data-availability.ce:para: Data will be made available on request.

ce:section.ce:para: For more than two decades, a group discrimination of organic–inorganic hybrid material called metal–organic frameworks (MOFs) has been raised in chemistry circles [1]. These solid crystalline porous material are formed from metal ions as organic nodes and ligand as linkers [2]. The dramatically increase of the researches in this area is due to the astonishing properties of these materials such as highly ordered crystal network [3], high and tunable surface area and porosity [4], different cavity geometries [5] and open metal sites [6]. Thus, MOFs and their derivations are increasingly used in areas like photocatalyst [7], gas adsorption [8], energy storage [9], drug delivery [10,11] and sensors [12,13]. The formation of MOFs from different metal ions and linkers led to except a number of plausible applications. Zn-based MOFs are one of subgroups of these metal–organic frameworks that Zn exists in their main structure as a node [14–16]. Zn-based MOFs can include nitrogen-containing ligand called zeolitic imidazolate frameworks or hydrophilic carboxylates containing ligand as linker which ZIF-11 and MOF-5 can respectively be counted as examples of Zn-based MOFs with basic and acidic linkers [17,18].

Quantum dots (QDs) have been greatly considered as a new generation of inorganic crystal semiconductors with dimension range from 1 to 100 nm [19]. These nanocrystals mostly include atoms from II to VI or III-V groups of elements in the periodic table and have smaller radius compared to that of Bohr atom [20,21]. Recently carbon-based quantum dots have been introduced as a new class of quantum dots which graphene quantum dots are the latest member of it [22,23]. They have received a great deal of attention due to their new properties like excellent photostability, high solubility, biocompatibility, photoluminescence adjustability in an extended range and non-toxicity status [22,24–28]. These extraordinary properties have turned them to an appropriate candidate for various applications, particularly optical applications in bio scopes, for example, the cytotoxicity of N-GQDs on Hela cells was evaluated by J. Su et al. and the results show that N-GQDs work well as a promising fluorescent probes in biological system. Also Ge et al. reported a novel PDT agent based on GQDs that enable GQDs to act as a multifunctional nanoplatform for the simultaneous imaging and highly efficient in vivo Photodynamic therapy (PDT) of cancer [22,24,26,29–32]. GQDs are zero dimensional luminescent graphene pieces with sizes smaller than 60 nm have significant edge effect, quantum confinement effect and non-zero band gap which caused they demonstrate enhanced performance compared to those two-dimensional graphene materials [33–35].

Nowadays, MOF-based composite material has been under increasing investigations in which an integration of MOFs with other components including ceramics, biopolymers, nanoparticles and protein has resulted in the development of new practical material with enhanced properties [36]. GQDs and MOF composites are the new types of these composites that can be considered as powerful bridge between carbon materials and metal–organic frameworks. In this regard, fabrication of graphene quantum dot and ZIF-8 composite [37] carried out and applied in LED [38] or chemo- and photothermal therapy [37].

The cyano group consists of a carbon atom triple-bonded to a nitrogen atom. Naturally, it is found in the roots of fruits and vegetables such as almonds, apricot kernels, millet sprouts, soybeans, spinach, lima beans, bamboo shoots and also found in some small animals such as millipedes, beetles and some rare species of butterflies. The presence of the cyanide group in the environment, which may be due to plastics production, gold and silver extraction or steel industry, may disrupt the mitochondrial oxygen transport chain by reducing oxygen consumption, causing dreadful health effects for the health of living organisms [39–42]. Therefore, methods to identify cyanides in the nature and their amount are required. For this purpose, various classical methods such as spectrophotometry [43], polarography [44], flow injection amperometry [45], potentiometry [46] and titrimetry [43,47] have been used, but their low detection limit, high cost and time consuming may limit their interest. In this report, we examine the use of the luminescence method, which could be faster, cheaper, more sensitive and accurate [48–51].

In previous work in our laboratory a sensor investigation on silver ions was performed by calix arene [52]. In this work, our effort was aiming at creating novel composites containing QD (GQD) as the latest non-toxic carbon nanoparticles with excellent optical properties and Zn-based MOFs (ZIF-11) which contains basic linkers (benzimidazole) as a porous structure and framework to encapsulate graphene quantum dot in order to help each other to enhance the sensor properties. Due to the presence of aromatic structure in the two constituent components of the composite, it seems that there is a strong π−π stacking interaction between GQD and benzimidazole in the MOF framework, which cause impressive enhancement in luminescence of composite in comparison with its individual components and facilitates the interactions of the surface charge transfer process and reduce the electron-hole pair recombination as identified from band gap calculation and PL studies [53]. For the first time, the importance and effect of composites as a sensor of cyanide ion which is one of the most dangerous toxins in the world and caused serious concerns to the environment (air and water contamination) have been investigated.

Citric acid, zinc acetate dihydtrate (98 %), methanol (99.5 %), ammonia solution (28–30 %), toluene (99.8 %) and benzimidazole were used as they received. In order to obtain ultrapure water, millipore system was used. Fluorescence measurements were carried out using a JASCO FP-6500 spectrophotometer. The FT-IR spectra were recorded on a Bruker IFS-88 spectrometer. X‐ray diffraction (XRD) patterns were recorded on a Malvern Panalytical. Thermal gravimetric analysis (TGA) carried out on a SDT Q600 instrument. Scanning electron microscope (SEM) images were recorded on a MIRA III. Zeiss-EM10C-100KV was used to take transmission electron microscope (TEM) images. Ultraviolet–visible (UV–vis) absorbance spectra of the solid samples were obtained by Perkin-Elmer Lambda-35. All measurements were operated at room temperature.

In order to synthesize graphene quantum dots, citric acid pyrolysis method [54] was used in which a glass container containing 2 g of citric acid was first incubated in the oil bath for 15 min at 200 °C. Then, the container was removed from the bath in order to cool it down to room temperature. Subsequently, under vigorously stirring conditions, aqueous NaOH solution (10 mg/mL) was dropwised until the solution reaches neutral pH of 7. In order to prevent the resulting suspension from aggregation, it was kept in a refrigerator. No more dilution was used for the final suspension.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Preparation of new composite of GQD@ZIF-11
ce:section.ce:para: To obtain the composite, 0.24 g (1.52 mmol) of benzimidazole was dissolved in 6.4 g of MeOH followed by addition of 9.2 g toluene and 2.4 g of NH4OH. Under stirring, 0.22 g (68.49 mmol) of zinc acetate dihydrate, 0.5 g of PVP and 13.14 mg/mL of GQD were dissolved in 3.2 g of MeOH. Both solutions were cooled to 18 °C and mixed together and then centrifuged at 10000 rpm for 5 min. Then, the solvent was decanted and the resulting solid washed with 15 mL of methanol (for three times) to complete the elimination of any trapped toluene molecule within ZIF-11 pores. Finally, the precipitate was collected by the centrifugation and then dried in an oven at 100 °C overnight (Scheme 1 ).

For the detection of cyanide ion, a dense solution of 20 different types of anions (10−5 M) and a 10−6 M solution of GQD@ZIF-11 composite was prepared. Then, 1.5 mL of the composite solution was added to the luminescence cell and the emission intensity was determined. In this way, 10, 20 and 50 μL of different anions were added to the composite respectively and each time the change in the intensity of the luminescence was observed. For the cyanide ion, this change is associated with a strong decrease in its luminescence compared to other ions. For interference effects, the interference test of cyanide ion was checked with 19 other anions, and the minimum amount of the interference of the anions in the luminescence of cyanide ion was appeared. The same procedure was applied for the other ions.

The quantum yield of the synthesized GQDs was determined according to below equation using quinine sulfate in 0.1 M H2SO4 as a standard sample.
Q =
Q R
I /
I R
A R
/ A
n 2
/
n R 2

In this equation Q is the quantum yield, I is the measured integrated emission intensity, n is the refractive index, and A is the absorbance at the excited wavelength. The “R” refers for sample with a known QY. According to the Table 1 , quantum yield of the GQDs was estimated to be about 27.4 %.

In FT-IR spectra the wide absorption band between 3100 and 3400 cm−1 and the intense absorption band at 1700 cm−1 are related to the stretching vibrations of OH and CO groups of carbonyl of GQDs (Figure S1) [55,56]. Also, at about 1400 cm−1 and 1100, the vibrations of OH and COC are related to the existing hydroxyl and acid groups on the surface of GQD, respectively [25]. For ZIF-11 sample, the existing bands of imidazole linkers at 1470 and 1610 cm−1 are assigned to CC stretching in the aromatic ring. The bands in the reign of 600–1500 cm−1 indicate the entire ring stretching or bending while the band at 1580 cm−1 demonstrates the stretching of CN [57]. Furthermore, in FT-IR spectrum of GQD@ZIF-11 composite, absence of OH stretching band of GQD can be a good reason for indicating the interactions between GQD and ZIF-11 [37].

The powder X-ray diffraction patterns of the bare GQD, pure ZIF-11 and the composite of these two components are shown in Figure S2. For the bare GQD, there is a broad and low intensity amorphous peak around 20° (2θ)[58,59] which in pure ZIF-11 such broadening is not observed and only crystalline pattern with sharp diffraction and high intensity peaks is indicated [57,60]. Interestingly, there is no significant change in the XRD pattern compared to ZIF-11, which indicates that graphene quantum dot is well dispersed in the ZIF-11 [37,38,61].

Morphology of GQD, ZIF-11 and GQD@ZIF-11 was investigated using SEM and TEM images. As can be seen in Fig. 1 , graphen quantom dot demonstrated spherical well-dispesed nanoparticles while ZIF-11 observed in about a rhombic dodecahedral morphology (Fig. 1a & b) [60]. The TEM image of the resulted composite of these two samples reveals some spherical shaped particles, which is a result of interaction between GQD nanoparticles with ZIF-11 nanocrystals and consequently it clearly demonstrates encapsulation of GQD nanoparticles in ZIF-11 (Fig. 1c).

TGA analyses of GQD, ZIF-11 and GQD@ZIF-11under N2 atmosphere at a heating rate of 5 °C min−1 are respectively shown in Fig. 2 . About 3 % of primary weight loss in the beginning of the thermogram of pure GQD is due to water deletion [62,63]. A great weight loss occurred from 200 to 800 °C with the amount of 77 % is related to the decomposition of the carbons in the GQD framework [64]. In thermogram of ZIF-11, about 2 % of weight loss was observed which is attributed to elimination of encapsulated toluene within the framework between 120 and 200 °C and then a long gradual plateau in its TGA at about 500–900 °C with about 68 % weight loss indicates the high thermal stability of ZIF-11 that is in agreement with other reports [65]. In thermogram of GQD@ZIF-11 composite, a weight loss with the amount of about 15 % at about 600–700 °C is related to decomposition of GQD inside the ZIF-11 and a long gradual plateau at about 700–800 °C is related to decomposition of ZIF-11 frameworks. According to these observations, it is confirmed that GQD nanoparticles were capsulated in ZIF-11 matrix which this information is in accordance with the previously obtained data [37].

Band gap feature of GQD, ZIF-11 and GQD@ZIF-11 composite is shown in Figure S3 which is calculated from UV–vis diffuse reflectance spectra (Figure S4) data according to the following formula [66]:
α h v
n
= A
hv -
E g
where α, h, ν, A, and Eg are absorption coefficient, Planck constant, light frequency, a constant and optical band gap energy, respectively. In this formula the value of n can be equal to 2 and 1/2 for direct and indirect band gaps. To obtain the band gap energy, we should find a straight line in (αhν) n versus photon energy graph. Extrapolating the straight part to the energy axis in (αhν) n = 0 demonstrates the band gap value. Therefore, by applying this equation, calculated value of Eg for GQD, ZIF-11 and GQD@ZIF-11 are 2.7, 2.75 and 2.65 eV, respectively. Furthermore, as shown in Figure 6., the shift of the absorption graph towards longer wavelengths is confirms that the band gap becomes smaller, which means that electron transfer requires less energy. Eventually, the synergic effect between GQD and ZIF-11decreases the band gap energy and recombination rate in GQD@ZIF-11compositeand increase electron transfer in GQD@ZIF-11 in comparison to GQDand ZIF-11 that is also a proof for the composite formation.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Optical properties of composite of GQD@ZIF-11
ce:section.ce:para: In the PL study of GQD and composite of GQD@ZIF-11, a sharp increase in the fluorescence intensity of the composite compared to GQD has been observed at 450 nm. As shown in Fig. 3 a & b, the PL spectrum of the composite shows a tiny blue shift of 5 nm in comparison to the GQD. Since the solvent effects can be observed for liquid PL, solid PL of the three GQD, ZIF-11 and GQD@ZIF-11 species were obtained in order to eliminate this effect (Fig. 3c). Solid PL spectrum exhibited that there is no emission peak for ZIF-11 while GQD@ZIF-11 showed strong intense blue shift peak about 65 nm which indicate that the interaction between GQDs and ZIF-11 is not a simple physical impact. These results provide reliable evidence for the successful encapsulation of GQD nanoparticles within ZIF-11 pores that indicates the interaction between polar and nonpolar functional groups existing on the surface of GQD with imidazole groups existing in ZIF-11 framework. Regarding this excellent luminescence characteristic demonstrated by GQD@ZIF-11, it can be considered as a new optical composite which can have further application in detection and sensing areas.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Photoluminescence detection of cyanide ion and competitive titration with different anions
ce:section.ce:para: To study the sensing capability of composite on different anions, luminescence is reliable and sensitive technique. PL behavior of 20 different anions on GQD@ZIF-11 composite was investigated (Fig. 4 c). We observed a very dramatic and considerable quench with 20 nm blue shift from 465 to 445 nm for cyanide ion and a calibration curve with a large range of linear relationship (LDR) between 0.15 and 30 μM are shown in Fig. 4a & b, respectively and based on 5 measurements made in 10−7 μM concentration, the RSD was obtained 0.95 %. Regarding the quenching mechanism caused by the presence of cyanide ion in the composite, it seems that cyanide ion has been able to prevent the effective interaction between functional groups on the surface of graphene quantum dot and benzimidazole, which has ultimately caused a decrease in luminescence.

Competitive effect of 19 other anions including (PO4 3−, CO3 2–, SCN−, NO2 –, ClO3 −, NO3 –, HCO3 –, CH3COO–, H2PO4 −, S2O8 2−, SO4 −, S2O4 2−, S2−, HS−, F−, OH–, Cl−, Br− and I−) with CN– (CN–: 1 × 10−6 M and other anions: 1 × 10−5 M) in methanol solution were studied. As can be seen in Fig. 4c, the initial emission at 460 nm wavelength for other anions is decreased by different amounts but none of them had significant quench and shift while only by increasing cyanide ion, considerable quench and shift was observed.

Limit of detection (LOD) of the sensor is calculated equal to 14.5 nM using the below equation:
LOD =
3 σ
/ m
Where σ is the standard intensity deviation and m is the slope between fluorescent variation intensity (I0-I) vs cyanide anion concentration variations. In order to calculate LOD, the intensity of the fluorescence of the composite has been measured five times in a row and the standard deviation of the blank solution was obtained. So far, many fluorescent sensors have been produced for the purpose of cyanide ion detection based on different materials which have been reported in the literatures. Table 2 , summarized some of these luminescent sensors with LOD and their operation mechanisms. The calculated LOD value obtained through fluorescence approach in this work (14.5 nM) is significantly superior to other reported sensors.

To obtain real solutions, cyanide was extracted from two different bitter seeds (apple seeds and bitter almonds). For this purpose, 4 g of pulverized pieces of bitter seeds were added to 100 mL MeOH and stirred and then sonicated for 1 h. Then, the residue was separated by filtration to obtain the target cyanide containing solutions. This special PL property of GQD@ZIF-11 composite was used again to detect CN− in real samples in order to achieve excellent results. As shown in Fig. 5 , photoluminescence spectra of the composite (0.1 μM) upon addition of extracted cyanide containing solution from different seeds (0.3 μM) has shown remarkable quench which may indicate the success of this sensor. Based on the measurements, 2.5 and 2.4 mg of cyanide were found per gram of the apple seeds and bitter almonds, respectively. The accuracy of the calculations was done by spiking specific concentrations of the standard cyanide solution to the unknown sample which the results are shown in Table 3 and the percentage of recovery rate is also shown in the table and it was within the reasonable range between 96.7 and 102.7 %.

We have developed a new, simple and sensitive photoluminescence sensor made from graphene quantum dots encapsulated in zeolitic imidazolate framework (ZIF-11) which it detected cyanide ion at very low concentration. The investigation of the competitive detection of an array of other 19 anions has demonstrated a quenching signal restricted to cyanide ion with, moreover, no effect on its detection limit. The interest of the procedure is highlighted by the low detection limit (14.5 nM) in comparison with existing photoluminescence cyanide assays, wide liner response ranges (LDR) from 0.15 to 30 μM, 0.95 % relative standard deviation and the non-toxicity of GQD in the sensor structure which could turn this sensor into a green sensor. Examination of real samples containing cyanide from bitter seeds has also shown remarkable results, which is a very good reflection of the sensor performance. As a significant perspective of this study, we can mention the wider use of this optical composite to identify the dangerous cyanide ion in the waste water of industrial factories and mines to help detecting and cleaning of the environment. Furthermore, it is possible to mention the construction of other photoluminescence composites using GQDs and other metal–organic frameworks to identify other anions or cations.

ce:conflict-of-interest.ce:para: The authors declare that they have no known competing financial interests or personal relationships that could have appeared to influence the work reported in this paper.

ce:acknowledgment.ce:para: We thank Chemistry & Chemical Engineering Research Center of Iran (CCERCI) for the support of these studies.

ce:section.ce:label: Appendix A
ce:section.ce:para: Supplementary data to this article can be found online at https://doi.org/10.1016/j.microc.2023.108494.

ce:section.ce:para:
The following are the Supplementary data to this article:
Supplementary data 1

sb:title.sb:maintitle: Highly sensitive and selective fluorescent sensor based on a multi-responsive ultrastable amino-functionalized Zn (II)-MOF for hazardous chemicals
